concept.headline: Maps store key-value pairs. Use atom keys with %{key: val} syntax and access with map.key. Update with %{map | key: new_val}. Maps are immutable — all operations produce new maps.
concept.explanation: Elixir maps are created with %{} literal. Atom keys use shorthand %{name: 'Alice'} (same as %{:name => 'Alice'}). Access atom keys with dot notation: map.name. Map.get(map, key) handles missing keys gracefully. The update syntax %{map | key: new_val} creates a new map with one key changed — it does NOT add new keys (use Map.put for that). All map operations are immutable.
concept.syntax:
# Create with atom keys
person = %{name: "Alice", age: 30}

# Access: dot notation (atom keys only)
person.name          # "Alice"
person.age           # 30

# Map.get — safe, returns nil or default if missing
Map.get(person, :name)        # "Alice"
Map.get(person, :email)       # nil
Map.get(person, :email, "n/a")  # "n/a"

# Update syntax (only existing keys!)
updated = %{person | age: 31}
# updated = %{name: "Alice", age: 31}
# person unchanged: %{name: "Alice", age: 30}

# Map.put — add or update a key
new = Map.put(person, :email, "a@b.com")

# Check key exists
Map.has_key?(person, :name)   # true
Map.has_key?(person, :phone)  # false

# Keys and values
Map.keys(person)    # [:name, :age]
Map.values(person)  # ["Alice", 30]
keyPoints: %{name: val} — atom key shorthand (most common Elixir style)
map.key — dot access for atom keys
%{map | key: new_val} — update existing key; produces new map
Map.get(map, key, default) — safe access with optional default

demo.intro: Watch a person map be created, accessed, and immutably updated.
demo.code:
person = %{name: "Alice", age: 30, role: "admin"}
IO.puts(person.name)
IO.puts(person.age)

IO.puts(Map.get(person, :role))
IO.puts(Map.get(person, :email, "none"))

updated = %{person | age: 31}
IO.puts(updated.age)
IO.puts(person.age)

enriched = Map.put(person, :email, "alice@example.com")
IO.puts(enriched.email)
IO.puts(Map.has_key?(enriched, :email))

explanation.what: person = %{name: 'Alice', age: 30, role: 'admin'} — map with 3 atom keys
explanation.why: %{key: val} syntax uses atom keys (shorthand for %{:key => val})
explanation.intent: Create a typical Elixir map with atom keys
explanation.misconception: Maps with atom keys (%{name: val}) are different from maps with string keys (%{'name' => val}). Atom keys support dot access (map.name); string keys do not (must use map['name'] or Map.get). Choose atom keys for structured data.

explanation.why: Dot notation works for atom keys
explanation.intent: Show dot access for atom key maps
explanation.misconception: Dot access raises KeyError if the key does not exist — unlike Map.get which returns nil. Use dot access when you are SURE the key exists; use Map.get when you need safe/default access.

explanation.intent: Confirm dot access for a second key
explanation.misconception: person.age returns the INTEGER 30. IO.puts converts it to the string '30' for output.

explanation.why: :role key exists; Map.get returns its value
explanation.misconception: Map.get(person, :role) is equivalent to person.role here. Map.get is preferred when the key might not exist or when you need a default value.

explanation.why: person has no :email key; the third argument is the default
explanation.intent: Show Map.get default value when key is missing
explanation.misconception: Without the default argument, Map.get(person, :email) returns nil — which IO.puts would print as empty string. The default parameter makes absent keys safe to handle.

explanation.what: %{person | age: 31} — creates new map with age changed to 31
explanation.why: | update syntax produces a new map; original unchanged
explanation.intent: Show immutable map update with | syntax
explanation.misconception: The | update syntax ONLY works for EXISTING keys. %{person | phone: '123'} raises KeyError because :phone is not in person. Use Map.put to add new keys.

explanation.why: updated has the new age value
explanation.intent: Show updated map has new value
explanation.misconception: updated is a SEPARATE new map — not a mutation of person.

explanation.intent: Prove original map is unchanged after update
explanation.misconception: This is the core of Elixir immutability. No function or operation ever modifies an existing value. person still has age: 30 after %{person | age: 31}.

explanation.why: | syntax cannot add new keys; Map.put can add OR update
explanation.intent: Show Map.put for adding new keys
explanation.misconception: Map.put both ADDS new keys and UPDATES existing keys. It creates a new map each time.

explanation.intent: Confirm Map.put added the new key
explanation.misconception: person still has no :email key — only enriched has it.

explanation.intent: Show Map.has_key? for key existence check
explanation.misconception: The ? suffix is a naming CONVENTION in Elixir for functions that return boolean. Map.has_key?, is_atom?, String.starts_with?, etc. It is NOT special syntax.

prediction.explanation: :c does not exist in m. Map.get with default 99 returns 99 when key is absent.

explanation.afterDemo: Maps are Elixir's primary key-value data structure. The atom-key shorthand %{name: val} and dot access make them feel like structs. The | update syntax and Map.put enforce immutability — you always get a new map, never modify in place. This predictability is a core strength of Elixir's functional design.
keyTakeaways: %{key: val} — atom key map. map.key for dot access.
%{map | key: new_val} — update existing key (KeyError if key absent).
Map.put adds/updates. Map.get with default handles missing keys safely.

practice.prompt: Create %{language: 'Elixir', year: 2012}. Use Map.put to add :creator => 'José Valim'. Then update :year to 2022 with | syntax. Print creator and year.
practice.hint: Three separate maps: original, after Map.put, after | update.

retryHints: %{lang2 | year: 2022} updates :year in the new map

summary.title: Maps — Complete
takeaways: %{key: val} atom-key syntax. map.key for dot access.
%{map | key: val} — immutable update. Map.put — add or update.
Map.get(map, key, default) — safe access. Map.has_key? — key check.
